learning_outcomes.description: Appreciate the long intellectual history that precedes the concept of the West.
Understand the diversity of opinion about what constitutes the West.
Appreciate the ability of creative works to explore questions of political consciousness and intervene in political debates.
Be able to evaluate competing interpretations of a text.
Recognise and understand the importance of conflict in the development of ideas, texts, and identities.
Be able to use evidence to evaluate arguments regardless of your own political beliefs and values.
Demonstrate an ability to communicate persuasively in written and oral genres.
Use artificial intelligence to generate representations of historical thought and assess their limitations.

assessments.due_date: Submissions are due Monday 10am of the week following the lecture on the text. Where Monday is a holiday/break, the deadline is Friday 3pm
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution
assessments.task_description:
Students are awarded up to 24 marks for participation and preparation. It is possible to be awarded a maximum of three marks for each seminar and there are 10 seminars. This means that the eight best grades will be chosen.

For each of the 10 seminars where we study texts, students must submit a written analysis that answers the set question and follows the model provided (see Blackboard-Assessment). Note: Each year some students struggle to follow the model and thereby lose marks.

Students who attend and actively participate will have their submissions graded based on the marking criteria (Blackboard). Non-attendance or inactive participation will result in a 0 being awarded for that week even if you submit the task. Expectations around summaries will be discussed in the Week 1 Lecture. The idea is that the lecture introduces the text and students then read it in preparation for the tutorial the following week.

Due date: Summaries must be submitted to Turnitin by 10am of the Monday following the lecture on the text. (Public holidays, semester breaks, and break weeks = due 3pm on the Friday following the lecture.)
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: Group submissions are due Monday 10am of the week following the lecture on the text. Where Monday is a holiday/break, the deadline is Friday 3pm. See Blackboard for group/text allocations
assessments.task_description:
In Week 1, each student will be allocated a week for which they will develop discussion questions for the corresponding seminar with a group of students from other seminars. This will involve:

1. Producing one comprehension question that can be answered by reading and understanding the text (the question should be neither easy nor perplexing), and one interpretive question that engages the logic of the text's argument/plot;  

2. Producing an answer sheet that states the essential elements of a correct answer to the comprehension question (see the Locke template on Blackboard in the Group Work assessment folder);

3. Independently verifying the comprehension answer to show its correctness; and producing a group response to the interpretive question that summarises the Zoom discussion;

4. Submitting a link to a Zoom/Teams recording of the group meeting where you discuss the final questions/answers and agree that they were feasible and accurate (see 3) and where the group tries to formulate a collective position on the interpretive question. The recording should also show the group agreeing that the submission is of a good standard, and noting any issues regarding individual contributions. 

Note: To record your discussion, login using your UQ account, select record to the Cloud, copy this link and then include it in your submission. For help finding the link: https://support.zoom.com/hc/en/article?id=zm_kb&sysparm_article=KB0067567#:~:text=Sign%20in%20to%20the%20Zoom,the%20meeting%20ID%20or%20topic.

5. Submitting 1-4 as a single document to Turnitin before Monday 10am of the week the text is discussed in seminars. (For example, Castiglione is the Week 3 Lecture and Week 4 Seminar, hence the Q&A submission is due Monday 10am of Week 4.) 

Note: Only one person from the group needs to submit, using the group's name, forwarding the submission receipt to the other members of the group. If a group member is non-responsive or does not participate professionally and competently, please bring this to my attention immediately. Individual grades will be adjusted where this occurs as noted in the marking criteria (Blackboard).
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit a single document to Turnitin before Monday 10am of the week the text is discussed in seminars. Only one person from the group needs to submit, using the group's name.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Students will choose two thinkers from this list: Machiavelli, Castiglione, Macaulay, Gibbon (for whom public domain editions exist). They must then produce a 500w dialogue using AI in which the two thinkers debate some aspect of (or concept from) Christendom/Europe/the West. See the instructions on Blackboard. This is worth 20% of your mark with your prompts etc. submitted as per the instructions. Note that abiding by copyright is a strict requirement and failure to do so will see your grade reduced to 0 for this part of the task.

Students will then perform source criticism on this dialogue (see instructions on Blackboard). This part is worth 80% of your mark.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will sign up to an approximate time to meet with the Course Coordinator in Week 2 of the exam period. This time and date will be treated as the time of the exam.

At the beginning of the assessment, the student will nominate one segment of the course's three (i.e. Courts & Republics/Grand Narrative/Self-Doubts) and the examiner will then nominate another of the two remaining segments.

They will then be asked the question: 'How has the West changed across these two periods?'. The student should speak in answer with reference to the course's texts (or set parts thereof) for 4-5 minutes (an alarm will sound at 4.30).

Two follow-up questions will then be asked that will be intended to allow the student to amplify/clarify their answer and demonstrate their mastery of the texts (answers should be approximately 2 minutes each). None of the questions will be deliberately difficult. 

We will build oral presentation skills and confidence throughout the tutorials, including a chance for an informal practice of the assessment with a peer in seminars toward the end of semester. See Blackboard for the marking criteria.
